I remember watching LOTR: Return of the King and that scene, that line "I am no man." And I was so freaking excited. I thought the next step was more movies like these, but with female protagonists. ....I didn't expect this. Why does money and politics have to ruin everything? 

All these franchises, including LOTR, they were so good...because they were taking a chance.  They did it because of the passion for the source material and passion for storytelling. 

Now it's passion for politics and clout chasing. So many times I heard the lines "First of its kind with this representation." for some new DEI project in my favorite genre, I just roll my eyes as I remember so. many. stories that did it already and did it better. That's how I know they did not do their homework, they do not care about the source material. They just want to "make a difference" and be the first, even though they were like a decade too late. replies.author: @Tarcus32
replies.text:
Most lore I’ve seen on Orcs in Middle Earth has them as either a creation of Morgoth as a joke of the Elvish race or Elves that were corrupted by Morgoth and used as his main force army. In this text, Orcs having families and raising young could make sense to a certain degree, however a key part of Orc culture that is consistent in nearly every use of them in fantasy is that they are a proud race who values strength over all else. For an Orc to complain about the suffering of their family would be something that would have that Orc killed by the others on the spot. 

My understanding of Tolkien is the Orcs used in Two Towers by Saruman were a creation of a the wizard himself, kind of like cloning, that would breed beasts meant for warfare. Meanwhile the Orcs under Sauron’s reign were more like the people of a nation inside Mordor, which would make it very possible and likely they would breed. Of course the writers had no clue how to do it properly.

author: @whocanmissu
text: I'm a woman in my fifties, and I used to enjoy movies regardless of whether the lead was a man or a woman—because they were genuinely entertaining. Even films with female leads were fantastic, like the Resident Evil series, Alien with Ellen Ripley, Thelma & Louise, and Kill Bill. They were powerful without being preachy. But nowadays, films are filled with a certain type of over-dominant female character, and the message often feels like a lecture. Movies just aren't fun anymore—they’ve become boring and even off-putting. I also don’t understand the obsession with pushing diversity into every corner of every movie. It’s called filmmaking, not a lecture hall.
